data.title: Ceres (TSE:3696) Margin Jump To 11.3% Puts Earnings Quality Debate In Focus
data.description:
Ceres (TSE:3696) just opened Q1 2026 with revenue of ¥9,521 million and basic EPS of ¥86.06. This comes in a context where trailing twelve month revenue is ¥31,495 million and EPS is ¥307.48, alongside very large year on year earnings growth that includes a ¥2.5 billion one off gain. Over the past reported periods, the company has seen revenue move from ¥7,487.73 million in Q4 2024 to ¥7,927.14 million in Q1 2025 and then to ¥9,521 million in Q1 2026. Quarterly EPS shifted from ¥55.10 to ¥67.39 and then to ¥86.06 as net income progressed from ¥634.24 million to ¥776.12 million and then ¥993 million. With current net margins at 11.3% compared with 2.1% last year, these results put profitability and the quality of those margins firmly in focus for investors.

See our full analysis for Ceres.

With the latest figures on the table, the next step is to set these margins and growth against the dominant stories around Ceres to see which narratives the numbers support and which they challenge.

See what the community is saying about Ceres

Margins Jump To 11.3% On Trailing Basis

  • On a trailing twelve month view, Ceres reports net income of ¥3,547 million on revenue of ¥31,495 million, which equates to an 11.3% net margin compared with 2.1% a year earlier.
  • What stands out for the bullish view is how this margin picture mixes core improvement with one off help, since:
    • Reported earnings over the last year rose by a very large multiple of about 7x, helped by a ¥2.5 billion one off gain included in that period.
    • Even with that boost, the move from a 2.1% to 11.3% margin suggests higher profitability on current operations that bullish investors often point to when arguing that the business is on a stronger footing.

Valuation Screens Cheap On 6.7x P/E

  • Ceres trades on a trailing P/E of 6.7x versus the JP Media industry at 15.4x and peer average at 11.1x. The current share price of ¥2,062 sits well below a DCF fair value of ¥6,110.21.
  • Consensus style bullish arguments about mispricing get strong backing from these gaps. Yet the data also asks how much is due to quality of earnings, because:
    • The share price discount sits alongside that very large earnings jump helped by the ¥2.5 billion one off gain, so part of the low P/E reflects earnings that are not purely recurring.
    • Revenue is forecast to grow about 15.1% per year while earnings are forecast to grow only about 0.2% per year, which cautious investors may see as a check on the optimistic view implied by the DCF fair value.

Strong Revenue Trend With Mixed Signals On Stability

  • Over the last reported quarters, revenue has moved from ¥7,487.734 million in Q4 2024 to ¥7,927.139 million in Q1 2025 and then to ¥9,521 million in Q1 2026, while trailing twelve month revenue is ¥31,495 million with forecast annual growth of about 15.1%.
  • Critics highlight that the quality and stability of this profile are just as important as the growth, and the numbers present a mixed picture, since:
    • Dividend history is described as unstable and the share price has been more volatile than the JP market over the past three months, which bears can point to when arguing that cash returns and price behaviour do not yet match the size of the trailing earnings jump.
    • At the same time, trailing net income of ¥3,547 million and the higher 11.3% margin show a much stronger profit base than the prior year, so the data does not purely support a bearish view of weak fundamentals.

data.title: McGrath RentCorp (MGRC) Margin Compression Challenges Bullish Rental Profitability Narratives
data.description:
McGrath RentCorp (MGRC) opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of about US$198.5 million and basic EPS of roughly US$1.10, while trailing twelve month revenue stood at US$947.4 million with EPS of about US$6.30. The company reported quarterly revenue of US$195.4 million and EPS of roughly US$1.15 in Q1 2025, followed by a peak of US$256.8 million in revenue and EPS of about US$2.02 in Q4 2025 before this latest quarter. This progression provides context for how investors may assess margins as the new year begins.

See our full analysis for McGrath RentCorp.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings print lines up with the widely followed narratives about McGrath RentCorp and where those stories get tested by the data.

See what the community is saying about McGrath RentCorp

Advertisement

Margins Compress as Net Profit Slips to 16.4%

  • Trailing 12 month net income of US$155.1 million on US$947.4 million of revenue works out to a 16.4% net margin, compared with 25.8% a year earlier on US$910.9 million of revenue and US$231.7 million of net income.
  • Consensus narrative points to recurring rental demand as a support for profitability, yet the drop in margin from 25.8% to 16.4% suggests cost pressure and segment softness are having a clear impact even while trailing revenue has risen from US$910.9 million to US$947.4 million.
    • Critics highlight weaker segment demand and higher operating costs in areas like Mobile Modular and Portable Storage, which lines up with lower margins despite trailing earnings per share of US$6.30 still being described as high quality.
    • Supporters who focus on long term rental adoption need to reconcile that positive theme with the recent compression in net margin, since that shift directly affects how much of each rental dollar converts to profit.

Share Price, P/E and Valuation Gap Stand Out

  • At a share price of US$110.55 and trailing EPS of about US$6.30, McGrath RentCorp trades on a P/E of 17.5x, below both the peer average of 27.3x and the US Trade Distributors industry at 23.4x, while a DCF fair value of US$138.15 and an analyst price target of US$144.60 both sit above the current price.
  • Consensus narrative talks up recurring rental revenue and long term growth potential, and the current valuation gap tests that view by suggesting the market is pricing the recent slowdown in forecast growth at 3.4% for revenue and 6.6% for earnings more cautiously than analysts who see about 30.8% upside to targets.
    • What stands out is that five year earnings grew at about 18.8% a year, yet forward growth forecasts are now in the mid single digits, which may explain why investors are not assigning peer level P/E multiples despite the premium implied by the DCF fair value.
    • At the same time, the 1.79% dividend yield and lower P/E versus peers give some support to the view that investors who accept slower forecast growth are not paying the same multiple as for faster growing trade distributors.

Quarterly EPS Trend and Balance Sheet Trade Off

  • Quarterly basic EPS moved from US$1.15 in Q1 2025 to US$2.02 in Q4 2025 and then to about US$1.10 in Q1 2026, while trailing EPS over the last four quarters sits at roughly US$6.30 and the company continues to carry a high level of debt alongside its 1.79% dividend yield.
  • Bears argue that softer demand in some rental segments, higher selling and general administrative expenses and reliance on existing fleet inventory, combined with that high debt load, could pressure both earnings and cash generation, and the recent margin decline and EPS pattern across the last four quarters give some support to that cautious view even as rental demand and diversification efforts underpin the longer term story.
    • Weaker utilization readings in areas such as Portable Storage and Mobile Modular have been highlighted as pressure points, which lines up with the move in trailing net margin from 25.8% to 16.4% on broadly similar sub US$1b revenue levels.
    • At the same time, the reliable dividend and history of five year earnings growth around 18.8% per year show why some investors still see room for returns if leverage is managed and segment demand stabilizes.
Skeptics focus on the margin compression and debt load, while others point to the valuation gap and rental model strengths, so it can be useful to see how the full bear case frames these same numbers before deciding how they fit your own view of the business.🐻 McGrath RentCorp Bear Case

Approach 1: Veradermics Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to what they might be worth in today’s dollars. It is essentially asking what a stream of future cash flows is worth right now.

For Veradermics, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $71.63 million. Analyst and model projections then move through a period of negative free cash flows, such as $69 million in 2026 and $95.50 million in 2027, before reaching a projected free cash flow of $123 million in 2030. Beyond the analyst window, Simply Wall St extrapolates cash flows out to 2035 using its own growth assumptions.

Combining all of these discounted cash flows results in an estimated intrinsic value of about $250.72 per share. Against a current share price around $99, the DCF output implies the stock trades at roughly a 60.5% discount to this estimate, which indicates a wide gap between price and modeled value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Veradermics.

Approach 2: Veradermics Price vs Book

For companies in pharmaceuticals, price to book, or P/B, can be a useful yardstick because it compares what you pay in the market with the accounting value of net assets on the balance sheet. For profitable, established businesses, investors often look for a P/B that reflects both the quality of those assets and the earnings they can support.

Growth expectations and risk also shape what feels like a normal P/B level. Faster growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher uncertainty can point to a lower one. This is why simple comparisons can be tricky.

Veradermics currently trades on a P/B of 30.09x, which sits well above the Pharmaceuticals industry average of 2.63x and the peer group average of 4.33x. In this case, Veradermics’ actual P/B of 30.09x is meaningfully higher than the Fair Ratio. This points to the shares screening as expensive on this measure.

    • which means anyone worried about a slower period ahead will likely pay close attention to whether the higher Q2 and Q3 EPS figures can be repeated or if results drift toward the lower end of this recent range.

Mixed valuation signals at 12.3x P/E

  • NANKAI trades on a P/E of 12.3x, below the JP market average of 14.4x and the peer average of 12.8x, but above the JP Transportation industry at 11.3x. The current share price of ¥2,985 also sits well above a DCF fair value of ¥1,012.88.
  • Critics highlight this gap between the ¥2,985 share price and the DCF fair value of ¥1,012.88 alongside a balance sheet where debt is not well covered by operating cash flow, and the figures add weight to that cautious angle because
    • the cheaper than market P/E can be seen against forecasts for a 2.3% per year earnings decline, which helps explain why the stock might trade below the broader JP market multiple,
    • while the higher multiple than the transportation industry and the DCF value sitting materially under the current price give investors a clear reason to stress test how much they trust the recent 11.7% earnings growth and 9.5% margin.

data.title: Infomart (TSE:2492) Net Margin Jump Reinforces Bullish Narratives Despite Premium P/E
data.description:
Infomart (TSE:2492) opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of ¥4.9 billion and basic EPS of ¥2.54, setting the tone for its latest set of numbers. Over recent quarters the company reported revenue of ¥4.3 billion in Q1 2025 and around ¥4.9 billion in Q1 2026, while basic EPS moved from ¥1.35 to ¥2.54, giving investors a clearer view of how the top and bottom lines are tracking into the new fiscal year as margins evolve.

See our full analysis for Infomart.

With the latest figures reported, the next step is to consider how this earnings run rate aligns with widely discussed views on Infomart's growth drivers, risks, and profitability profile.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Margins Backed by 11.5% Net Profit

  • On a trailing basis, Infomart generated ¥22,26.24 million in net income on ¥19,415.11 million in revenue, which works out to an 11.5% net profit margin compared with 4.6% a year earlier.
  • What stands out for the bullish view is that trailing Basic EPS of ¥9.69 is supported by quarterly EPS prints between ¥1.86 and ¥2.88 across the past four reported quarters,
    • This range, from Q2 2025 EPS of ¥2.40 to Q3 2025 EPS of ¥2.88, shows earnings per share staying comfortably above the ¥1.35 level seen in Q1 2025.
    • Bulls who argue that recent margin strength is becoming more established can point to Q1 2026 net income of ¥610 million on revenue of ¥4,903 million sitting in line with that trailing 11.5% margin profile.

Earnings Growth Far Outruns Revenue

  • Over the last 12 months the data show revenue running at roughly 13% annual growth while earnings expanded by 193.5%, with trailing net profit rising from ¥655 million to ¥2,226.24 million.
  • Critics highlight that such a gap between earnings and revenue growth can be hard to repeat, even with the current numbers looking strong,
    • They may question how durable a move from a 4.6% to 11.5% net margin is when Q1 2026 revenue of ¥4,903 million is close to Q4 2025 revenue of ¥5,014 million, yet net income moved from ¥421 million to ¥610 million in those quarters.
    • At the same time, the trailing Basic EPS increase from ¥2.89 to ¥9.69 over six TTM snapshots is exactly what the bullish side points to as evidence that earnings growth is doing more of the work than top line alone.

Premium P/E and DCF Gap

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 47.5x compared with roughly 12.5x for the broader JP market and around 13x for the Professional Services industry, while the current price of ¥397 sits above the DCF fair value estimate of ¥152.76.
  • Bears argue that this combination of a premium multiple and a DCF value below the market price leaves little room for disappointment,
    • The difference between ¥397 and the ¥152.76 DCF fair value, along with a P/E that is roughly 3 to 4 times peer levels, is the core of the cautious view even with trailing earnings growth of 193.5%.
    • They also point to shareholder dilution over the past year and share price volatility above the JP market as additional reasons why such a high multiple could be sensitive to any slowdown from the current ~13% revenue growth rate.
